1:
"Let every soul be subject unto the higher powers. For there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God." The witnesses note the command to be subject to the civil authorities. Chrysostom notes Paul makes much of this subject, setting subjects under their rulers — to show that Christ introduced his laws not for the subversion of the commonwealth, but for the better ordering of it, teaching men not to take up unnecessary wars. The witnesses note Paul charging every soul to be subject to the higher powers, since there is no power but of God and the powers that be are ordained of him; the duty of submission to civil government, grounded in the divine institution of authority — the magistrate's power deriving from God, government an ordinance of God for the ordering of human society; the divine warrant of civil authority. The witnesses note be subject. The witnesses note the powers ordained of God.

The witnesses note the command to be subject to civil authority — grounded in the divine institution of government, ordained of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the command to be subject to authority. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of submission to civil government, grounded in the divine institution of authority. The witnesses note the command: "let every soul be subject unto the higher powers" — every soul (every person, including Christians) is to be subject to the higher powers (the civil authorities, the government); the subjection; the command; the submission; the duty to authority. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the aim: Paul makes much of this, setting subjects under their rulers — to show that Christ's laws are not for the subversion of the commonwealth but for its better ordering, teaching men not to take up unnecessary wars; the aim; the gospel not seditious; the better ordering; the Christian no rebel. The witnesses press the divine institution: "there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God" — all authority is from God; the powers that exist are ordained (appointed, instituted) by him; the divine institution; the authority from God; the government ordained; the magistrate's power derived from God. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine warrant of authority: civil authority is a divine ordinance — God instituted government for the ordering of human society, the restraint of evil, the protection of the good; the divine warrant; the ordinance of God; the government instituted; the authority God's; "by me kings reign" (Proverbs 8:15). The witnesses observe the ground of submission: the believer submits to authority because it is ordained of God — the submission to the magistrate a submission to God's ordinance; the ground; the divine institution the warrant; the obedience to authority as to God's appointment; the conscience bound. The expositors note the implied limit: though the powers are ordained of God, the obedience is in things lawful — for God is the higher authority, and where the magistrate commands against God, the apostles' rule holds, "we ought to obey God rather than men" (Acts 5:29); the limit; the obedience in the lawful; God the higher authority; the submission bounded by conscience toward God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the be subject, the powers ordained of God, and the divine warrant of authority. The verse thus records the command to be subject to authority — teaching the duty of submission to civil government, grounded in its divine institution.
2:
"Whosoever therefore resisteth the power, resisteth the ordinance of God: and they that resist shall receive to themselves damnation." The witnesses note that resisting authority resists God's ordinance. Gill notes the one who resists the power is he who denies that there ought to be such order, despises and opposes it, and will not be subject to it in any form; Barnes notes it means those who rise up against government itself, seeking anarchy and confusion. The witnesses note Paul declaring that whoever resists the power resists the ordinance of God, and the resisters shall receive judgment; the gravity of rebellion against lawful authority — since authority is God's ordinance (v.1), to resist it (in the sense of rising against government itself) is to resist God, and brings judgment; the warrant against anarchy and lawless rebellion. The witnesses note resisteth the ordinance of God. The witnesses note shall receive damnation.

The witnesses note that resisting authority resists God's ordinance — the gravity of rebellion against lawful government, bringing judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the gravity of resisting authority. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since authority is God's ordinance, to resist it (rising against government itself) is to resist God, and brings judgment. The witnesses note the resisting: "whosoever therefore resisteth the power" — Gill notes the resister is one who denies there ought to be such order, despises and opposes it, will not be subject; Barnes notes those who rise up against government itself, seeking anarchy; the resisting; the rebellion; the opposing of authority; the lawless. The expositors observe the resisting of God: "resisteth the ordinance of God" — to resist the power is to resist the ordinance of God — for authority is God's institution (v.1); the resisting of God's ordinance; the rebellion against God's appointment; the opposing of the divine institution; the resistance to God. The witnesses press the judgment: "they that resist shall receive to themselves damnation" — the resisters shall receive judgment (condemnation, punishment) — from the magistrate, and from God; the judgment; the punishment; the condemnation; the resisters judged; the penalty of rebellion. The expositors draw the lesson on the gravity of rebellion: rebellion against lawful authority is grave — for it resists God's ordinance; the gravity; the seriousness of rebellion; the resisting of God; the rebellion against the divine institution; the grave sin. The witnesses observe the warrant against anarchy: the verse warrants against anarchy and lawless rebellion — the rising against government itself condemned; the against anarchy; the order upheld; the rebellion forbidden; the lawless judged; the warrant for order. The expositors note the careful sense: the resisting condemned is the rising against government itself (anarchy), not the lawful, conscientious refusal to obey a command against God (which is obedience to the higher authority, Acts 5:29); the careful sense; the anarchy condemned, the conscientious refusal allowed; the distinction; the rebellion and the conscience distinguished. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the resisteth the ordinance of God, the shall receive damnation, and the gravity of rebellion. The verse thus records that resisting authority resists God's ordinance — teaching the gravity of rebellion against lawful government, bringing judgment.
3:
"For rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil. Wilt thou then not be afraid of the power? do that which is good, and thou shalt have praise of the same:" The witnesses note the function of rulers — a terror to evil, not good. Gill notes Paul anticipates the objection that rulers are terrible, and removes it by observing that governors neither do nor ought to be a terror to those who do good in a civil sense; the well-behaved have nothing to fear. The witnesses note Paul declaring that rulers are not a terror to good works but to evil, and that the one who does good shall have praise of the power; the proper function of civil government — to restrain and punish evil, and to encourage and commend the good; the magistrate the guardian of order, fearful to the evildoer, no terror to the upright. The witnesses note not a terror to good works. The witnesses note do good, have praise.

The witnesses note rulers a terror to evil, not good — the function of government to restrain evil and encourage good; the upright need not fear.

So the witnesses make the verse the function of rulers. The lesson the expositors draw is the proper function of civil government — to restrain and punish evil, and to encourage and commend the good. The witnesses note the function: "rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil" — Gill notes governors neither do nor ought to be a terror to those who do good (civilly); the rulers a terror to evil, not good; the function; the restraint of evil; the terror to the evildoer; the proper office. The expositors observe the removal of the objection: Gill notes Paul anticipates the objection that rulers are terrible, and removes it — the well-behaved have nothing to fear; the objection removed; the rulers not fearful to the good; the upright safe; the fear groundless for the good. The witnesses press the encouragement of the good: "wilt thou then not be afraid of the power? do that which is good, and thou shalt have praise of the same" — the one who does good need not fear the power, but shall have praise (commendation, approval) of it; the encouragement; the praise of the good; the upright commended; the well-doer approved; the good rewarded by authority. The expositors draw the lesson on the proper function of government: government is to restrain and punish evil and to encourage and commend the good; the proper function; the office of the magistrate; the guardian of order; the punisher of evil, the encourager of good; the office defined. The witnesses observe the magistrate the guardian of order: the magistrate is the guardian of public order — fearful to the evildoer, no terror to the upright; the guardian; the order kept; the evil restrained; the good protected; the magistrate's office; the public order. The expositors note the implied standard for rulers: the verse describes the magistrate's proper function (a terror to evil, not good) — the standard by which rulers should act; the proper function the standard; the ideal of government; the magistrate's duty; the rule for rulers (though some fail it). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not a terror to good works, the do good and have praise, and the function of government. The verse thus records rulers a terror to evil, not good — teaching the proper function of civil government, to restrain evil and encourage the good.
4:
"For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain: for he is the minister of God, a revenger to execute wrath upon him that doeth evil." The witnesses note the magistrate as God's minister, bearing the sword. Gill notes the ruler is a minister of God's appointing and commissioning, acting under him, a kind of vicegerent, appointed for the people's good — natural, moral, civil, and spiritual. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the ruler is the minister of God to the believer for good, but a terror and avenger to the evildoer, for he bears not the sword in vain; the magistrate God's servant for the public good, armed with the power of the sword (the right of punishment, including capital), an avenger executing God's wrath on the evildoer; the divine commission and the just authority of the magistrate. The witnesses note the minister of God for good. The witnesses note beareth not the sword in vain.

The witnesses note the magistrate God's minister for good, bearing the sword — the just authority to punish, an avenger on the evildoer.

So the witnesses make the verse the magistrate as God's minister bearing the sword. The lesson the expositors draw is that the magistrate is God's servant for the public good, armed with the power of the sword to punish the evildoer. The witnesses note the minister for good: "he is the minister of God to thee for good" — Gill notes the ruler is a minister of God's appointing, a kind of vicegerent, appointed for the people's good (natural, moral, civil, spiritual); the minister of God; the servant for good; the divine commission; the magistrate God's servant; the office for good. The expositors observe the terror to the evildoer: "if thou do that which is evil, be afraid" — the evildoer is to fear the magistrate; the terror to evil; the fear of the wrongdoer; the magistrate fearful to the evil; the dread of the lawbreaker. The witnesses press the bearing of the sword: "he beareth not the sword in vain" — the magistrate bears the sword — the symbol of the power of punishment, including the power over life (capital punishment); the sword; the power of punishment; the right of the sword; the authority to punish; the sword not borne in vain. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the sword: the magistrate's bearing of the sword warrants the power of punishment — including, the witnesses note, capital punishment (the sword the instrument of death); the power of the sword; the right of punishment; the just authority; the lawful penalty; the magistrate's coercive power. The witnesses observe the avenger of God's wrath: "the minister of God, a revenger to execute wrath upon him that doeth evil" — the magistrate is God's avenger, executing God's wrath on the evildoer; the avenger; the executing of wrath; the minister of justice; the wrath executed; God's justice through the magistrate. The expositors note the contrast with private revenge: the magistrate executes God's wrath on the evildoer (lawful, public justice), whereas the private believer is not to avenge himself (Romans 12:19); the contrast; the public justice and the private forbearance; the magistrate's office and the believer's; the lawful avenging and the private renouncing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the minister of God for good, the bearing of the sword, and the magistrate God's avenger. The verse thus records the magistrate God's minister bearing the sword — teaching the just authority of the magistrate to punish the evildoer as God's servant for the public good.
5:
"Wherefore ye must needs be subject, not only for wrath, but also for conscience sake." The witnesses note the twofold ground of subjection. Barnes notes the reasons for subjection were two: that government is appointed by God, and that violation of the laws would expose to punishment; Gill notes there is a necessity of subjection, magistracy being God's ordinance for the good of men. The witnesses note Paul concluding that believers must be subject, not only for wrath (to avoid the magistrate's punishment) but also for conscience' sake (because it is God's ordinance); the higher motive of Christian obedience — not mere fear of penalty, but conscience toward God, who ordained the authority; the obedience that springs from conscience, not only from fear. The witnesses note for wrath. The witnesses note for conscience' sake.

The witnesses note the twofold ground of subjection — not only for wrath (the penalty) but for conscience' sake (toward God who ordained it).

So the witnesses make the verse the twofold ground of subjection. The lesson the expositors draw is the higher motive of Christian obedience — not mere fear of penalty, but conscience toward God. The witnesses note the necessity: "ye must needs be subject" — Gill notes there is a necessity of subjection, magistracy being God's ordinance for the good of men; the necessity; the must; the duty; the subjection required; the obligation. The expositors observe the first ground: "not only for wrath" — Barnes notes one reason is that violation exposes to punishment; the for wrath; the fear of the penalty; the avoiding of the magistrate's wrath; the lower motive; the prudential ground. The witnesses press the higher ground: "but also for conscience sake" — the believer is to be subject also for conscience' sake — because it is God's ordinance, and conscience binds him to obey; the for conscience; the higher motive; the conscience toward God; the obedience from conscience; the binding of conscience. The expositors draw the lesson on the higher motive: the Christian obeys not merely from fear of penalty, but from conscience toward God — because God ordained the authority; the higher motive; the conscience; the obedience to God's ordinance; the not-mere-fear; the conscientious obedience. The witnesses observe the two grounds: the two grounds — wrath (the penalty) and conscience (toward God); the prudential and the conscientious; the fear and the conscience; the lower and the higher; the twofold ground; the both motives. The expositors note the dignity of conscientious obedience: the obedience for conscience' sake is the higher, nobler obedience — springing from reverence for God, not mere dread of punishment; the dignity; the conscientious obedience; the reverence for God; the noble motive; the obedience of conscience. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for wrath, the for conscience' sake, and the higher motive of obedience. The verse thus records the twofold ground of subjection — teaching the higher motive of Christian obedience, not only for wrath but for conscience toward God.
6:
"For for this cause pay ye tribute also: for they are God's ministers, attending continually upon this very thing." The witnesses note the payment of tribute as a duty. Gill notes that to show our subjection to the higher powers, and as a proof of it, we pay tribute to support them in their office and dignity; and this is done not for fear of trouble, but for conscience' sake — the payment of taxes not a mere matter of prudence, but a duty. The witnesses note Paul declaring that for this cause believers pay tribute also, since the rulers are God's ministers attending continually on the work of government; the duty of paying taxes — the support of the government that serves the public good, the magistrates being God's ministers devoted to their office; the obligation of tribute grounded in the divine appointment of the rulers. The witnesses note pay ye tribute. The witnesses note God's ministers.

The witnesses note the duty of paying tribute — the support of the government, the rulers being God's ministers devoted to their office.

So the witnesses make the verse the payment of tribute. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of paying taxes — the support of the government that serves the public good, grounded in the divine appointment of the rulers. The witnesses note the duty: "for for this cause pay ye tribute also" — Gill notes that to show our subjection, and as a proof of it, we pay tribute; the payment of tribute; the taxes; the duty; the support of the government; the tribute owed. The expositors observe the conscience in it: Gill notes this is done not for fear of trouble, but for conscience' sake — the payment of taxes not a mere matter of prudence, but a duty; the conscience; the duty, not mere prudence; the conscientious payment; the obligation; the tribute as a duty. The witnesses press the ground — God's ministers: "for they are God's ministers, attending continually upon this very thing" — the rulers are God's ministers, attending continually on the work of government; the ground; the rulers God's ministers; the devotion to the office; the continual attendance; the magistrates' labour. The expositors draw the lesson on the support of government: the tribute supports the government — the rulers who devote themselves to the public service; the support; the maintenance of the government; the magistrates upheld; the public service funded; the rulers supported. The witnesses observe the rulers' devotion to the office: the rulers attend "continually" on the work of government — devoting themselves to the public service; the devotion; the continual labour; the office attended; the magistrates' work; the service rendered. The expositors note the obligation grounded in the divine appointment: the obligation of tribute is grounded in the divine appointment of the rulers — God's ministers, to be supported; the obligation; the divine appointment the ground; the rulers God's ministers; the support due; the duty grounded in God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the pay ye tribute, the God's ministers, and the duty of supporting the government. The verse thus records the payment of tribute — teaching the duty of paying taxes, the support of the government, the rulers being God's ministers devoted to their office.
7:
"Render therefore to all their dues: tribute to whom tribute is due; custom to whom custom; fear to whom fear; honour to whom honour." The witnesses note the rendering of all dues. Matthew Henry notes we are here taught a lesson of justice and charity — to render to all their due, as a debt, not a gift. Gill notes we should render to all princes, magistrates, and officers, from the supreme to the lowest, whatever belongs to them. The witnesses note Paul charging believers to render to all their dues — tribute, custom, fear, and honour, each to whom it is due; the comprehensive duty of giving everyone his due — taxes and customs to the government, reverence and honour to those in authority; the justice that pays every obligation, the rendering of dues as debts owed, not favours given. The witnesses note render to all their dues. The witnesses note tribute, custom, fear, honour.

The witnesses note rendering to all their dues — tribute, custom, fear, honour — the justice that pays every obligation as a debt owed.

So the witnesses make the verse the rendering of all dues. The lesson the expositors draw is the comprehensive duty of giving everyone his due, the justice that pays every obligation as a debt. The witnesses note the command: "render therefore to all their dues" — Matthew Henry notes a lesson of justice — to render to all their due, as a debt, not a gift; Gill notes to render to all princes and officers whatever belongs to them; the rendering; the dues; the obligations paid; the giving of each his due; the debts rendered. The expositors observe the dues enumerated: "tribute to whom tribute is due; custom to whom custom; fear to whom fear; honour to whom honour" — the dues — tribute (direct taxes), custom (duties on goods), fear (reverence), honour (esteem) — each to whom it is due; the enumeration; the taxes and the reverence; the material and the moral dues; the obligations listed. The witnesses press the material dues: tribute and custom (the taxes and duties) are to be rendered to the government — the material support; the taxes; the customs; the material obligations; the dues of money; the government supported. The expositors draw the lesson on the moral dues: fear and honour (reverence and esteem) are to be rendered to those in authority — the moral obligations; the reverence; the honour; the respect due; the esteem owed; the moral dues paid. The witnesses observe the dues as debts: the dues are rendered as debts owed, not favours given — for justice requires the payment of every obligation; the dues as debts; the justice; the obligations owed; the debts paid; the giving of due. The expositors note the comprehensiveness: the rendering is to "all" — every obligation to every person to whom it is due; the comprehensive duty; the all; the every due; the full justice; the complete rendering. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the render to all their dues, the tribute, custom, fear, honour, and the justice that pays every obligation. The verse thus records the rendering of all dues — teaching the comprehensive duty of giving everyone his due, the justice that pays every obligation as a debt.
8:
"Owe no man any thing, but to love one another: for he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law." The witnesses note the debt of love that is never discharged. Gill notes that, from the payment of dues to magistrates, Paul proceeds to a general exhortation to discharge all debts — to owe no man anything, but make good all obligations, whether civil or natural. The witnesses note Paul charging believers to owe no man anything but to love one another, for love fulfils the law; the duty of discharging every debt (leaving no obligation unpaid), with the one exception of love — the perpetual debt that is always owing and never fully discharged, and which, in loving the neighbour, fulfils the whole law toward him; the debt of love. The witnesses note owe no man anything. The witnesses note love fulfils the law.

The witnesses note the debt of love never discharged — owe no man anything but love; love the fulfilling of the law toward the neighbour.

So the witnesses make the verse the debt of love. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of discharging every debt, with the one exception of love — the perpetual debt that fulfils the law. The witnesses note the discharging of debts: "owe no man any thing" — Gill notes Paul proceeds to a general exhortation to discharge all debts — civil and natural; the owing of nothing; the debts paid; the obligations discharged; no debt unpaid; the just discharge. The expositors observe the exception: "but to love one another" — the one debt always owing is love; the exception; the debt of love; the perpetual debt; the love always owed; the one obligation never fully discharged. The witnesses press the perpetual debt of love: love is the debt that is always owing — never fully paid, ever to be rendered; the perpetual debt; the love always owed; the obligation that remains; the debt never discharged; the ever-owing love. The expositors draw the lesson on love fulfilling the law: "he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law" — to love the neighbour is to fulfil the law toward him — for love does all that the law requires toward the neighbour; the fulfilling of the law; the love that does the law's duty; the law fulfilled in love; the love comprehending the law; the second table fulfilled. The witnesses observe the discharge of all other debts: the believer is to discharge all other debts — to leave no civil or natural obligation unpaid; the honesty; the just discharge; the debts paid; the obligations met; the integrity in obligations. The expositors note the harmony of justice and love: the verse joins justice (owe no man anything) and love (the perpetual debt) — the believer just in all his dealings and loving toward all; the harmony; the justice and the love; the honest and the loving; the integrity and the charity. The witnesses note whether this forbids all borrowing: the witnesses note "owe no man anything" does not forbid all lawful borrowing or credit, but the leaving of debts unpaid, the failing of obligations; the not-forbidding lawful credit; the duty to pay; the debts discharged when due; the obligation met. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the owe no man anything, the love fulfils the law, and the debt of love. The verse thus records the debt of love — teaching the duty of discharging every debt, with the perpetual exception of love, which fulfils the law toward the neighbour.
9:
"For this, Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not kill, Thou shalt not steal, Thou shalt not bear false witness, Thou shalt not covet; and if there be any other commandment, it is briefly comprehended in this saying, namely, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." The witnesses note the commandments summed up in love. Gill notes Paul reckons up the laws of the second table (toward the neighbour), to show that so far as a man loves his neighbour, he fulfils the law. The witnesses note Paul gathering the commandments — against adultery, killing, stealing, false witness, coveting — and summing them all in the one saying, "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself"; the second table of the law (the duties to the neighbour) comprehended in love, the one command that includes all, for love does no wrong and all good to the neighbour; love the sum of the law. The witnesses note the commandments enumerated. The witnesses note love thy neighbour as thyself.

The witnesses note the commandments summed up in "love thy neighbour as thyself" — the second table comprehended in the one law of love.

So the witnesses make the verse the commandments summed in love. The lesson the expositors draw is that the second table of the law (the duties to the neighbour) is comprehended in the one command of love. The witnesses note the enumeration: "Thou shalt not commit adultery... kill... steal... bear false witness... covet" — Gill notes Paul reckons up the laws of the second table; the commandments enumerated; the duties to the neighbour; the prohibitions; the second table; the laws toward the neighbour. The expositors observe the summing: "if there be any other commandment, it is briefly comprehended in this saying, namely, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself" — all the commandments toward the neighbour are comprehended in the one saying, "love thy neighbour as thyself"; the summing; the comprehending; the one command; the law summed; the love that includes all. The witnesses press the comprehension in love: the second table is comprehended in love — for love does no wrong to the neighbour (no adultery, no killing, no stealing, no false witness, no coveting) and all good; the comprehension; the love including all; the one law; the duties summed; the love comprehending the commandments. The expositors draw the lesson on love as the sum: love is the sum of the law toward the neighbour — the one principle that fulfils all the particular commands; the sum; the principle; the love that fulfils; the one comprehending the many; the law summed in love. The witnesses observe the standard — "as thyself": "love thy neighbour as thyself" — the standard of the love is "as thyself"; the measure; the loving as oneself; the standard; the self the measure; the neighbour loved as oneself. The expositors note the omission of the first table: Gill notes Paul omits the first commandment (toward God) here, reckoning the second table (toward the neighbour) — for the context is the duty to others; the omission; the second table; the duties to the neighbour; the love to man (the love to God assumed); the focus on the neighbour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the commandments enumerated, the love thy neighbour as thyself, and the second table summed in love. The verse thus records the commandments summed up in love — teaching that the second table of the law is comprehended in the one command, "love thy neighbour as thyself."
10:
"Love worketh no ill to his neighbour: therefore love is the fulfilling of the law." The witnesses note that love does no ill and so fulfils the law. Gill notes the man who truly loves his neighbour will contrive no ill against him nor do any to him — not injure his person, defile his bed, defraud his substance, hurt his character, or covet his goods; but do him all the good he can. The witnesses note Paul concluding that love works no ill to the neighbour, and therefore love is the fulfilling of the law; the proof that love fulfils the law — since love does no wrong to the neighbour (the negative duties) and all good (the positive), it accomplishes all the law requires toward him; love the fulfilling of the law, the principle that does what the commandments demand. The witnesses note love works no ill. The witnesses note love the fulfilling of the law.

The witnesses note love working no ill to the neighbour, and so being the fulfilling of the law — the principle that does all the law requires.

So the witnesses make the verse love as the fulfilling of the law. The lesson the expositors draw is that love, working no ill and all good to the neighbour, accomplishes all the law requires toward him. The witnesses note the working no ill: "love worketh no ill to his neighbour" — Gill notes the one who loves his neighbour will contrive no ill nor do any — not injure his person, defile his bed, defraud his substance, hurt his character, or covet his goods; the no ill; the love that harms not; the neighbour unharmed; the negative duties fulfilled; the no wrong done. The expositors observe the positive good implied: love not only works no ill, but does all the good it can (Gill); the positive good; the love that benefits; the good done; the positive duties; the neighbour helped; the all good. The witnesses press the conclusion: "therefore love is the fulfilling of the law" — since love works no ill and all good, love is the fulfilling of the law; the conclusion; the love fulfilling; the law accomplished; the fulfilment; the love that does the law. The expositors draw the lesson on love fulfilling the law: love fulfils the law toward the neighbour — for it does all the law requires (no wrong, all good); the fulfilling; the love accomplishing the law; the principle that does the commandments; the law summed and fulfilled in love; the love the fulfilment. The witnesses observe the principle behind the commands: love is the principle behind the commands — the commandments forbid the wrongs that love would never do, and require the goods that love delights to render; the principle; the love behind the law; the commandments expressing love; the law the form, love the substance; the principle and the precepts. The expositors note love the spring of obedience: love is the spring of obedience — the heart that loves the neighbour keeps the law toward him; the spring; the love the source; the obedience flowing from love; the law kept by love; the love the motive. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the love works no ill, the love the fulfilling of the law, and the principle that does all the law requires. The verse thus records love as the fulfilling of the law — teaching that love, working no ill and all good to the neighbour, accomplishes all the law requires.
11:
"And that, knowing the time, that now it is high time to awake out of sleep: for now is our salvation nearer than when we believed." The witnesses note the call to awake, the time being short. Chrysostom notes that, having given the fitting commands, Paul again thrusts them on to good works in consideration of what is pressing — the time of judgment is at the doors; as he wrote to the Corinthians, "the remaining time is short." The witnesses note Paul urging believers, knowing the time, to awake out of sleep, for salvation is nearer than when they first believed; the motive of the shortness of time and the nearness of the consummation, the call to rouse from spiritual drowsiness to vigilance and diligence, since the day of full salvation draws ever nearer; the urgency of the redeemed life. The witnesses note knowing the time. The witnesses note salvation nearer.

The witnesses note the call to awake out of sleep, the time being short and salvation nearer — the urgency of the redeemed life.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to awake. The lesson the expositors draw is the motive of the shortness of time and the nearness of the consummation, rousing the believer to vigilance. The witnesses note the knowing the time: "knowing the time, that now it is high time to awake out of sleep" — Chrysostom notes Paul thrusts them to good works in consideration of what is pressing — the time of judgment at the doors; the knowing the time; the high time; the awaking; the rousing; the season understood. The expositors observe the awaking from sleep: "to awake out of sleep" — the believer is to awake out of sleep — the spiritual drowsiness, the sloth, the carnal security; the awaking; the rousing; the vigilance; the shaking off of slumber; the alertness. The witnesses press the nearness of salvation: "for now is our salvation nearer than when we believed" — salvation (the full, final salvation, the consummation) is nearer than when they first believed; the nearness; the salvation approaching; the consummation drawing near; the day at hand; the salvation closer. The expositors draw the lesson on the motive of the shortness of time: the shortness of time and the nearness of the consummation are the motive to diligence — since the day draws near, the believer should be vigilant, diligent, awake; the motive; the time short (1 Corinthians 7:29); the urgency; the nearness rousing; the consummation spurring. The witnesses observe the call to vigilance: the believer is to rouse from drowsiness to vigilance and diligence — the time being short, the salvation near; the vigilance; the diligence; the watchfulness; the alertness; the rousing to action; the awake life. The expositors note the urgency of the redeemed life: the redeemed life is urgent — the time short, the day near, the salvation approaching; the urgency; the pressing time; the redeemed diligence; the life that redeems the time (Ephesians 5:16); the urgent walk. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the knowing the time, the awake out of sleep, and the nearness of salvation. The verse thus records the call to awake — teaching the motive of the shortness of time and the nearness of the consummation, rousing the believer to vigilance.
12:
"The night is far spent, the day is at hand: let us therefore cast off the works of darkness, and let us put on the armour of light." The witnesses note the figure of the night and the day. Gill notes "the night" is best understood of the present time of life (called by the Jews "this world is like to the night"), and the day the coming glory; the night far spent, the day at hand. The witnesses note Paul, by the figure of the passing night and the approaching day, calling believers to cast off the works of darkness and put on the armour of light; the present life the night nearly gone, the day of glory at hand, the call to lay aside the deeds of darkness (sin) and to put on the armour of light (the graces and the holy life) as those who belong to the coming day; the casting off and the putting on. The witnesses note the night far spent. The witnesses note armour of light.

The witnesses note the night far spent, the day at hand — casting off the works of darkness and putting on the armour of light.

So the witnesses make the verse the figure of the night and the day. The lesson the expositors draw is the call to lay aside the deeds of darkness and put on the armour of light, as those who belong to the coming day. The witnesses note the figure: "the night is far spent, the day is at hand" — Gill notes "the night" is best understood of the present life (this world like the night), the day the coming glory; the night far spent; the day at hand; the figure; the passing night; the approaching day. The expositors observe the casting off: "let us therefore cast off the works of darkness" — the believer is to cast off the works of darkness — the deeds of sin, the carnal practices, the unfruitful works; the casting off; the laying aside; the deeds of darkness; the sins put off; the works of night. The witnesses press the putting on: "and let us put on the armour of light" — the believer is to put on the armour of light — the graces, the holy life, the defenses of righteousness; the putting on; the armour; the light; the graces donned; the holy life; the armour of righteousness. The expositors draw the lesson on belonging to the day: as those who belong to the coming day (the children of light), believers are to cast off the works of darkness and put on the armour of light; the belonging to the day; the children of light; the day's people; the holiness that befits the day; the light against the darkness. The witnesses observe the armour of light: the "armour of light" — the graces and defenses of the holy life, by which the believer both adorns and defends himself; the armour; the light; the defense and the adornment; the holy graces; the armour of righteousness (Ephesians 6:11). The expositors note the urgency of the figure: the figure presses the urgency — the night nearly gone, the day at hand; therefore the time to cast off darkness and put on light; the urgency; the night spent; the day near; the time to rise; the urgent change. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the night far spent, the cast off the works of darkness, and the put on the armour of light. The verse thus records the figure of the night and the day — teaching the call to lay aside the deeds of darkness and put on the armour of light, as those who belong to the coming day.
13:
"Let us walk honestly, as in the day; not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying." The witnesses note the call to walk honestly, avoiding the works of darkness. Gill notes that, being under the day of the gospel, with the daystar risen in our hearts and exposed to the view of all in broad daylight, we ought to walk decently, not naked, but clothed with the armour of light. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to walk honestly (decently, becomingly) as in the day, not in rioting and drunkenness (excess), nor chambering and wantonness (uncleanness), nor strife and envying (discord); the honest, holy walk that befits the children of the day, the six works of darkness named in three pairs — intemperance, impurity, and contention — to be shunned; the walk in the light. The witnesses note walk honestly. The witnesses note the works of darkness named.

The witnesses note the call to walk honestly as in the day — shunning intemperance, impurity, and contention; the walk in the light.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to walk honestly. The lesson the expositors draw is the honest, holy walk that befits the children of the day, shunning the works of darkness. The witnesses note the walk: "let us walk honestly, as in the day" — Gill notes that, being under the day of the gospel, exposed to all in broad daylight, we ought to walk decently; the honest walk; the decent walk; the walk as in the day; the becoming conduct; the holy walk. The expositors observe the works shunned: "not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying" — the six works of darkness, in three pairs — rioting and drunkenness (intemperance, excess), chambering and wantonness (impurity, uncleanness), strife and envying (contention, discord); the works named; the sins shunned; the darkness avoided; the three pairs; the vices forbidden. The witnesses press the three categories: the works of darkness fall in three categories — intemperance (rioting, drunkenness), impurity (chambering, wantonness), and contention (strife, envying); the three; the intemperance, impurity, contention; the categories; the sins of excess, of lust, of discord; the threefold darkness. The expositors draw the lesson on the walk that befits the day: the honest, holy walk befits the children of the day — who, in the light, shun the works of darkness; the befitting walk; the children of the day; the holiness; the walk in the light; the decent conduct; the day's people. The witnesses observe the exposure in the day: as in the day, the believer's walk is exposed to view — therefore he is to walk honestly, not in the secret sins of the night; the exposure; the open walk; the day's light; the conduct seen; the walk in the open; the no hidden sins. The expositors note the contrast of the night and day works: the works of darkness (intemperance, impurity, contention) are the deeds of the night; the honest walk is the deed of the day; the contrast; the night and the day works; the darkness and the light; the deeds opposed; the two walks. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the walk honestly, the works of darkness named, and the walk that befits the day. The verse thus records the call to walk honestly as in the day — teaching the honest, holy walk that befits the children of the day, shunning intemperance, impurity, and contention.
14:
"But put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make not provision for the flesh, to fulfil the lusts thereof." The witnesses note the great command to put on Christ. Gill notes putting on the Lord Jesus Christ as a man puts on his clothes — his righteousness, the best robe, covering and adorning; and also the putting on of Christ in the daily walk, in imitation and the exercise of grace. The witnesses note Paul crowning the exhortation — put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh to fulfil its lusts; the positive and the negative — to put on Christ (his righteousness for justification, and his graces and likeness for the daily walk) and to give no place to the flesh, making no provision for its lusts; the verse of Augustine's conversion, the whole duty of the believer in Christ. The witnesses note put on the Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note make no provision for the flesh.

The witnesses note the command to put on Christ and make no provision for the flesh — the positive and negative of the redeemed life.

So the witnesses make the verse the command to put on Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the positive (putting on Christ) and the negative (making no provision for the flesh) of the redeemed life. The witnesses note the putting on of Christ: "put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ" — Gill notes putting on Christ as a man puts on his clothes — his righteousness (the best robe, covering and adorning), and also Christ in the daily walk (imitation and the exercise of grace); the putting on of Christ; the clothing with Christ; the righteousness and the likeness; the Christ put on. The expositors observe the two senses of putting on Christ: putting on Christ embraces his righteousness (for justification, the robe that covers) and his graces and likeness (for the daily walk, the imitation of Christ); the two senses; the righteousness and the imitation; the justification and the sanctification; the Christ for covering and for pattern; the double putting on. The witnesses press the negative: "make not provision for the flesh, to fulfil the lusts thereof" — the believer is to make no provision for the flesh — giving no place, no occasion, no indulgence to its lusts; the no provision; the flesh denied; the lusts not fulfilled; the no place to sin; the flesh starved. The expositors draw the lesson on the positive and negative: the verse joins the positive (put on Christ) and the negative (no provision for the flesh) — the whole duty of the believer; the positive and negative; the Christ on, the flesh denied; the putting on and the putting off; the double duty; the redeemed life. The witnesses observe the no provision for the flesh: to make no provision for the flesh is to give it no occasion, no fuel, no indulgence — not planning for its gratification; the no provision; the no fuel; the flesh unfed; the lusts denied; the occasions of sin avoided; the flesh given no place. The expositors note Augustine's conversion: the witnesses note this verse was the means of Augustine's conversion — read in the garden, it pierced him and turned him from his sins to Christ; the famous conversion; Augustine; the verse that converted; the power of the word; the historic conversion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the put on the Lord Jesus Christ, the no provision for the flesh, and the positive and negative of the redeemed life. The verse thus records the command to put on Christ — teaching the positive (putting on Christ, his righteousness and likeness) and the negative (making no provision for the flesh) of the redeemed life, the verse of Augustine's conversion.
